• Download Rmd

Data Visualization Project 1
Infographic

Coffee Chain Comparison

Frank Marino
Northeastern University
INSH 5302: Information Design and Visual Analytics

February 08, 2026

Introduction

Is Starbucks considered ‘luxury’ coffee?

Coffee brands often carry cultural meanings beyond the drinks they sell. Starbucks is frequently perceived as a more “premium” or “luxury” coffee brand, while Dunkin’ is often praised for its affordability and convince. This project explores whether those perceptions are reflected in the socioeconomic characteristics of the communities where these stores are located.

Using county-level data comparing Starbucks and Dunkin’ locations across 6 counties in Massachusetts, this analysis examines differences in median household income and store distribution. Visualizations including boxplots, bar charts, and summary tables are used to highlight income patterns and cost differences between the two brands. Together, these visuals help tell a data-driven story about how brand identity, geography, and economic context intersect in everyday consumer choices.

Data

Load Libraries and Packages

rm(list = ls())
require(moderndive)
require(dplyr)
require(tidyselect)
require(mosaic)
require(tibble)
require(sqldf)
require(tidyr)
require(ggpubr)
require(modeest)
DD_SB <- DD_vs_SB

Description of data

R Package: moderndive

Data set: DD_vs_SB

This data set compares the geographic distribution of Dunkin’ and Starbucks locations across 6 Massachusetts counties. It includes county-level demographic variables such as median household income and population, along with the number of stores for each brand. This data set was chosen because it allows for a clear comparison between two well-known coffee brands and supports an exploration of how brand presence relates to local socioeconomic conditions.

Load and Clean Data

dd_sb_clean <- DD_SB %>%
  filter(
    shops >= 1,
    !is.na(median_income),
    !is.na(shop_type)
  )
dd_sb_clean <- dd_sb_clean %>%
  select(-FIPS)

Counties with missing income values or zero store counts were removed to ensure that all analyses reflected meaningful comparisons between existing Dunkin’ and Starbucks locations.

Preview Data

head(dd_sb_clean,10)
county median_income population shop_type shops
Bristol 121607 5419 starbucks 1
Bristol 114769 3999 dunkin_donuts 1
Bristol 72042 5408 dunkin_donuts 1
Bristol 126840 5626 dunkin_donuts 2
Bristol 103542 6720 dunkin_donuts 1
Bristol 82361 3078 dunkin_donuts 1
Bristol 70308 6562 dunkin_donuts 2
Bristol 94340 4410 dunkin_donuts 4
Bristol 94340 4410 starbucks 1
Bristol 75967 9107 dunkin_donuts 2

Variables

names(dd_sb_clean)
## [1] "county"        "median_income" "population"    "shop_type"    
## [5] "shops"

The variables featured in this data visualization project are:

  1. County - The county in which the store is located.
  2. Median_income - The median household income of the country in which the store is located.
  3. Population - The population of the county in which the store is located.
  4. Shop_type - Whether the store is a Dunkin Donuts or a Starbucks.
  5. Shops - The number of locations for each shop_type in the county.

Data Analysis

Required Analysis

Chart/Graph

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(dd_sb_clean, aes(x = shop_type, y = median_income, fill = shop_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = shop_type), width = 0.2, alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "starbucks" = "#006241",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "#F5821F"
    ),
    labels = c(
      "starbucks" = "Starbucks",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "Dunkin'"
    ),
    name = "Store Type"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "starbucks" = "#006241",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "#F5821F"
    ),
    labels = c(
      "starbucks" = "Starbucks",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "Dunkin'"
    ),
    name = "Store Type"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    "starbucks" = "Starbucks",
    "dunkin_donuts" = "Dunkin'"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Median Income by Store Type",
    x = "Store Type",
    y = "Median Income"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

cup_cost <- data.frame(
  Brand = c("Dunkin'", "Dunkin'", "Starbucks", "Starbucks"),
  Drink = c("Brewed Coffee", "Brown Sugar Shaken Espresso", "Brewed Coffee", "Brown Sugar Shaken Espresso"),
  Size = c(14, 24, 16, 16),
  Cost = c(3.39, 5.83, 3.19, 6.69), # Costs obtained from app ordering
  CostPerOz = c(0.24, 0.24, 0.20, 0.42) # Calculations
)

ggplot(cup_cost, aes(x = Drink, y = CostPerOz, fill = Brand)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("$", CostPerOz)), 
            vjust = -0.5, position = position_dodge(0.9), color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Starbucks" = "#006241",
    "Dunkin'" = "#F5821F"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Cost per oz of Dunkin' vs Starbucks Drinks",
       y = "Cost per oz ($)", x = "Drink") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 15),        # axis tick labels
    axis.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 15),       # axis titles
    plot.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 15)        # plot title
  ) #colors changed for export to canva

Summary Statistics

favstats(~median_income, data =dd_sb_clean)
min Q1 median Q3 max mean sd n missing
12490 57728.5 76890.5 96161 226181 79393.68 31616.78 654 0 
median_income_summary <- dd_sb_clean %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean = mean(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    mode = mlv(median_income, method = "mfv")[1],  # pick first mode
    sd = sd(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    range = max(median_income, na.rm = TRUE) - min(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    min = min(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q1 = quantile(median_income, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q3 = quantile(median_income, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



median_income_by_store <- dd_sb_clean %>%
  group_by(shop_type) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean = mean(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    mode = mlv(median_income, method = "mfv")[1],  # pick first mode
    sd = sd(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    range = max(median_income, na.rm = TRUE) - min(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    min = min(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(median_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q1 = quantile(median_income, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q3 = quantile(median_income, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  mutate(shop_type = case_when(
    shop_type == "dunkin_donuts" ~ "Dunkin'",
    shop_type == "starbucks" ~ "Starbucks"
  ))
  table_plot <- ggtexttable(median_income_by_store,
                          rows = NULL,
                          theme = ttheme(
                            "light", 
                            base_size = 12,
                            colnames.style = colnames_style(
                              fill = "grey90", 
                              color = "black", 
                              face = "bold"
                            ),
                            tbody.style = tbody_style(
                              fill = c("#F5821F", "#006241"),  # Dunkin/Starbucks row colors
                              color = "white"
                            )
                          )) # Kind of ugly, maybe won't include

Additional Analyses

wilcox.test(median_income ~ shop_type, data = dd_sb_clean)
## 
##  Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction
## 
## data:  median_income by shop_type
## W = 29812, p-value = 0.0001162
## alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0
tapply(dd_sb_clean$median_income, dd_sb_clean$shop_type, median)
## dunkin_donuts     starbucks 
##         75461         86250

A Wilcoxon rank-sum test revealed a statistically significant difference in local median income between Starbucks and Dunkin locations (W = 29,812, p = 0.00012). Starbucks locations were situated in areas with higher median income (median = $86,250) compared to Dunkin locations (median = $75,461).

median_df <- dd_sb_clean %>%
  group_by(shop_type) %>%
  summarise(MedianIncome = median(median_income, na.rm = TRUE))

# Bar plot with median labels and p-value
ggplot(median_df, aes(x = shop_type, y = MedianIncome, fill = shop_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("$", format(MedianIncome, big.mark=","))),
            vjust = -.5, color = "black", size = 5) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = max(median_df$MedianIncome) + 5000, 
           label = "Wilcoxon p = 0.00012", size = 5, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "starbucks" = "#006241",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "#F5821F"
    ),
    labels = c(
      "starbucks" = "Starbucks",
      "dunkin_donuts" = "Dunkin'"
    ),
    name = "Store Type"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(
    "starbucks" = "Starbucks",
    "dunkin_donuts" = "Dunkin'"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Median Income by Store Type",
    x = "",
    y = "Median Income ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "black"),
    axis.title = element_text(color = "black"),
    plot.title = element_text(color = "black", face = "bold", size = 16),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# colors were changed to white for canva presentation. 
sum_dunk <- sum(dd_sb_clean$shops[dd_sb_clean$shop_type == "dunkin_donuts"])
sum_star <- sum(dd_sb_clean$shops[dd_sb_clean$shop_type == "starbucks"])
store_counts <- dd_sb_clean %>%
  group_by(county, shop_type) %>%
  summarise(TotalShops = sum(shops, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = shop_type, values_from = TotalShops, values_fill = 0) %>%
  arrange(county)
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'county'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
table_plot <- ggtexttable(store_counts, 
                          rows = NULL,
                          theme = ttheme("light", base_size = 12))
table_plot

References

Include any references that you might have used to construct your graphic. This may include, but is not limited to citations for: graphics used, coding techniques, data collection, R-Markdown/RStudio documentation, etc. Delete this when complete.

  1. moderndive: Statistical Inference via Data Science.
  2. Dunkin’ and Starbucks mobile ordering applications (pricing data).
  3. https://www.starbucks.com/responsibility/sourcing/coffee/

All done!

Knit the completed R Markdown file as a HTML document (click the “Knit” button at the top of the script editor window) and upload it to the submission portal on Canvas.
